mixed.question: What is the world record for the most languages spoken in a single speech, set by polyglot Ziad Fazah during a televised challenge?
mixed.fact: Fazah claimed fluency in 58 languages, though later live tests showed he struggled with several of them.

mixed.question: In 2025, a new Guinness World Record was set for the largest human-made living structure. What was it?
options: A coral reef restoration wall in Indonesia
A moss-covered skyscraper facade in Singapore
A vertical forest tower in Milan
A living bamboo bridge in India
mixed.fact: The Indonesian reef restoration project surpassed 600 meters, built using biorock technology powered by low-voltage electricity.

mixed.question: True or False: The record for the world's longest fingernails (ever) exceeded 28 feet in total length across both hands.
mixed.fact: Lee Redmond grew her nails for nearly 30 years, reaching a combined 28 feet 4 inches before a car accident.

mixed.question: Who holds the record for surviving the highest fall without a parachute, after their plane broke apart mid-flight in 1972?
mixed.fact: Vesna Vulović survived a fall of 33,333 feet when her Yugoslav Airlines plane disintegrated over Czechoslovakia.

mixed.question: Both Abraham Lincoln and Charles Darwin were born on the exact same date. What was it?
mixed.fact: Two men who reshaped biology and democracy were born the same day, on opposite sides of the Atlantic.

mixed.question: Which composer died in 1827 and which famous scientist was born the same year — both profoundly shaping their fields?
mixed.fact: Beethoven died on March 26, 1827, the same year Joseph Lister — father of antiseptic surgery — was born.

mixed.question: In a bizarre historic coincidence, three separate U.S. presidents died on July 4th. Which president was NOT among them?
mixed.fact: Adams and Jefferson both died on July 4, 1826 — exactly the 50th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence.

mixed.question: Which country's capital is the closest to the equator while sitting at over 2,800 meters (9,200 feet) above sea level?
mixed.fact: Quito sits just 25 km from the equator at 2,850 meters, making it the highest official capital nearest the equator.

mixed.question: Which European country has more land area than the entire United Kingdom but a population under 400,000?
mixed.fact: Iceland covers about 103,000 km² versus the UK's 94,000 km², yet has roughly 380,000 people.

mixed.question: The city of İstanbul straddles two continents, but which other major city also spans two continents across a waterway?
mixed.fact: Atyrau is split by the Ural River, the traditional Europe-Asia boundary, making it a true transcontinental city.

science.question: What is the name of the recently discovered type of brain cell that is a hybrid between a neuron and an astrocyte, announced in 2024-2025 research?
science.fact: These cells release glutamate like neurons but look like astrocytes, rewriting our understanding of brain signaling.

science.question: Which neurotransmitter, best known for its role in reward, has been shown to play a critical role in encoding time perception in the basal ganglia?
science.fact: When dopamine levels drop, time feels like it's passing more slowly — explaining why boredom feels endless.

science.question: True or False: The human brain can detect a single photon of light under laboratory conditions.
options: Only after dark adaptation of 24+ hours
science.fact: A 2016 study confirmed humans can consciously detect a single photon, pushing vision to quantum limits.

science.question: What surprising phenomenon occurs in 'split-brain' patients when an object is shown only to the left visual field?
options: They see the object in color but can't name it
They can verbally name it but can't draw it
They can pick it up but can't verbally name it
They lose all awareness of the object entirely
science.fact: The right hemisphere recognizes the object but lacks language centers, so the hand knows what the mouth cannot say.

science.question: What is the value of the mathematical constant known as the 'golden ratio' (phi), approximately?
science.fact: Phi is the only number whose square equals itself plus one: φ² = φ + 1.

science.question: How many distinct ways can you arrange the vertices of an icosahedron into rotational symmetries (the order of its rotation group)?
science.fact: The icosahedral rotation group has 60 elements and is isomorphic to the alternating group A₅.

science.question: Which number is both a Fibonacci number and a triangular number, and is the largest known number with both properties below 1 million?
science.fact: Only three numbers are both Fibonacci and triangular: 0, 1, 3, 21, and 55 — surprisingly few overlap.

science.question: Which evolutionary phenomenon describes species on separate continents independently evolving remarkably similar body forms, such as marsupial and placental 'moles'?
science.fact: Marsupial moles and placental moles are genetically distant but nearly identical in form — evolution's copycat trick.

science.question: What counterintuitive finding did 2025 genomic studies reveal about Neanderthal DNA in modern humans?
options: It has been completely eliminated by natural selection
Some Neanderthal gene variants are being positively selected for in current populations
It only persists in European populations
It contributes nothing functional to modern biology
science.fact: Certain Neanderthal immune genes are actively increasing in frequency today, still helping us fight infections.

science.question: Which organism famously challenges traditional tree-of-life classification because it acquired roughly 8% of its genome through horizontal gene transfer from bacteria, fungi, and plants?
science.fact: Bdelloid rotifers haven't reproduced sexually for millions of years, instead stealing genes from other organisms to adapt.

history.question: Which Soviet cosmonaut was originally scheduled to fly Vostok 1 as backup, and would have been the first human in space if Gagarin had been scrubbed?
history.fact: Titov was only 25, making him the youngest person to fly in space when he later flew Vostok 2.

history.question: What was the name of the secret Soviet crewed lunar lander spacecraft, which never flew with cosmonauts aboard?
history.fact: The LK lander carried only one cosmonaut, unlike Apollo's two-person Lunar Module.

history.question: In 2025, NASA's Artemis II mission made history as the first crewed flight beyond low Earth orbit since 1972. How many astronauts were aboard?
history.fact: Artemis II carried four astronauts on a lunar flyby, including the first Canadian in deep space.

history.question: True or False: The first living creatures to travel around the Moon and return safely to Earth were two Soviet tortoises aboard Zond 5 in 1968.
history.fact: The tortoises lost about 10% of their body weight but were otherwise healthy after splashdown.

history.question: Which radical journalist's newspaper 'L'Ami du peuple' was so inflammatory that he had to hide in the Paris sewers to avoid arrest?
history.fact: Marat's chronic skin disease, possibly from hiding in sewers, forced him to work from his bathtub.

history.question: During the French Revolution, what surprisingly practical reason led to the adoption of the metric system in 1795?
options: To unify over 800 different units of measurement used across France
To spite the British Imperial system
To simplify tax collection from the church
history.fact: Pre-revolutionary France had roughly 250,000 different local units of weights and measures.

history.question: Who was the last person guillotined during the Reign of Terror, executed the day after ordering countless deaths himself?
history.fact: Robespierre's jaw was shattered by a gunshot the night before, so he screamed when the executioner removed his bandage.

history.question: Which of Henry VIII's wives was secretly married to the king for approximately four months before it was publicly announced?
history.fact: Henry and Anne Boleyn married secretly in January 1533, but it wasn't announced until April.

history.question: How many of the six wives of Henry VIII actually outlived him?
history.fact: Anne of Cleves and Catherine Parr both survived Henry, with Anne of Cleves outliving him by 10 years.

history.question: Which Tudor-era law made it technically treasonous to predict the death of the monarch, punishable by execution?
history.fact: Predicting the king's death was considered 'imagining' it, which legally counted as plotting treason.

popculture.question: Which 2000s reality TV show was originally developed as a scripted drama before being reworked into an unscripted format?
popculture.fact: Laguna Beach was initially pitched as a scripted teen soap before MTV reimagined it as a 'reality' show.

popculture.question: What was the default ringtone on the Motorola RAZR V3, one of the best-selling phones of the 2000s?
popculture.fact: The iconic 'Hello Moto' jingle became so ubiquitous that it appeared in over 130 million phones worldwide.

popculture.question: In 2025, which long-dormant 2000s social platform briefly relaunched with a nostalgia-driven campaign targeting millennials?
popculture.fact: Bebo attempted yet another comeback in 2025, leaning heavily on 2000s nostalgia and retro design elements.

popculture.question: True or False: The original iPod Shuffle (2005) had no screen whatsoever, and Apple marketed this as a feature called 'life is random.'
options: It had a small monochrome screen
popculture.fact: Apple boldly removed the screen entirely and used the tagline 'Life is random' to sell the screenless design.

popculture.question: Which 1980s pop star's music video was the first to cost over $1 million to produce?
popculture.fact: Thriller cost roughly $500K initially but ballooned past $1 million — unprecedented for a music video in 1983.

popculture.question: Who voiced the character of Venkman in the 1986 animated series 'The Real Ghostbusters' instead of Bill Murray?
popculture.fact: Ironically, Lorenzo Music also voiced Garfield — and Bill Murray later voiced Garfield in the 2004 film.

popculture.question: Which 1980s arcade game was so popular in Japan that it caused a national shortage of 100-yen coins?
popculture.fact: Space Invaders' 1978-80 craze was so intense that Japan's government had to mint extra 100-yen coins.

popculture.question: What was the original character limit for a single tweet when Twitter launched in 2006?
popculture.fact: The 140-character limit was based on SMS's 160-character cap, leaving 20 characters for the username.

popculture.question: Which social media platform was the first to introduce the 'Stories' format that disappears after 24 hours?
popculture.fact: Snapchat launched Stories in October 2013 — Instagram famously copied the feature three years later.

popculture.question: How many monthly active users did TikTok reportedly surpass on its platform globally by early 2025?
popculture.fact: TikTok crossed the 2 billion monthly active user milestone despite facing potential bans in several countries.

geography.question: Which country shares land borders with the most nations, totaling 14 neighbouring countries?
geography.fact: China and Russia are tied at 14 land neighbours, but China's include tiny borders with Afghanistan and Bhutan.

geography.question: True or False: Lesotho and Vatican City are the only two countries in the world completely surrounded by a single other country.
options: False — San Marino is also completely surrounded by one country
False — Monaco is also completely surrounded by one country
False — Andorra is also completely surrounded by one country
geography.fact: San Marino is entirely enclosed by Italy, just like Vatican City — making three such enclaved nations.

geography.question: Following a 2025 border demarcation agreement, which two Central Asian nations resolved a decades-long territorial dispute over their Fergana Valley enclaves?
geography.fact: Kyrgyzstan and Uzbekistan finalized exchange of Fergana Valley enclaves after 30+ years of negotiations.

geography.question: Which African country is bordered by exactly one other nation, making it the continent's only single-border state?
geography.fact: The Gambia is almost entirely swallowed by Senegal, with only a short Atlantic coastline breaking the encirclement.

geography.question: Which European microstate has Italian as its sole official language despite not bordering Italy?
geography.fact: San Marino is entirely surrounded by Italy, so it definitely borders Italy — the trick is none qualify!

geography.question: Which European country contains over 180,000 islands, the most of any nation on the continent?
geography.fact: Sweden's official count exceeds 220,000 islands, though most are tiny uninhabited rocky outcrops in its archipelagos.

geography.question: What is the only European capital city that straddles two continents?
geography.fact: Istanbul spans Europe and Asia across the Bosphorus, though Turkey's official capital Ankara is entirely in Asia.

geography.question: Which South American country has the highest average elevation, with much of its population living above 3,000 meters?
geography.fact: Bolivia's administrative capital La Paz sits at roughly 3,640 meters, making it the world's highest capital city.

geography.question: Suriname is the smallest country in South America by area. What is its official language?
geography.fact: Suriname is the only sovereign nation in South America where Dutch is the official language, a colonial legacy.

geography.question: Which South American country contains the world's driest non-polar desert yet also has high-rainfall temperate rainforests?
geography.fact: Chile's Atacama Desert gets virtually zero rainfall, while its southern Valdivian forests receive over 4,000mm annually.

sports.question: Which swimmer became the first person to break 46 seconds in the 100m freestyle, doing so at the 2024 Paris Olympics?
sports.fact: Pan Zhanle shattered the record with 46.40s, winning by nearly a full body length.

sports.question: True or False: Olympic marathon swimming (open water 10km) athletes are permitted to consume food from floating platforms during the race.
options: Only with referee permission per lap
sports.fact: Swimmers grab carbohydrate drinks from feeding poles on pontoons without stopping, a unique sight in the sport.

sports.question: What unusual rule exists in competitive water polo regarding players' feet?
options: Players must wear special webbed footwear
Players are not allowed to touch the bottom of the pool
Goalkeepers must keep one foot on the wall
Players must tread water with alternating feet only
sports.fact: Water polo players tread water the entire match — touching the pool bottom results in a turnover foul.

sports.question: Which country has won the most Olympic gold medals in synchronized swimming (artistic swimming) history?
sports.fact: Russia dominated artistic swimming for decades, winning gold at every Olympics from 2000 through 2016.

sports.question: How many FIFA World Cup tournaments did Italy win before being eliminated in the group stage for the first time in 2010?
sports.fact: Italy won four World Cups (1934, 1938, 1982, 2006) yet crashed out in the 2010 group stage as defending champions.

sports.question: Which player scored the fastest hat-trick in FIFA World Cup history, netting three goals in just 7 minutes?
sports.fact: Hungary's László Kiss scored three goals between the 69th and 76th minutes against El Salvador in 1982.

sports.question: Which country hosted the 1962 FIFA World Cup despite suffering a massive earthquake just two years earlier?
sports.fact: Chile hosted despite the 1960 Valdivia earthquake, the strongest ever recorded at 9.5 magnitude.

sports.question: Who is the only golfer to have won all four major championships on both the regular PGA Tour and the Champions (Senior) Tour?
sports.fact: Gary Player won 9 regular majors and all 4 senior majors, a feat no other golfer has matched.

sports.question: At which golf major did a 46-year-old Phil Mickelson become the oldest major championship winner in 2021?
sports.fact: Mickelson beat the previous record held by Julius Boros, who won the 1968 PGA Championship at age 48 — wait, Boros was older!

sports.question: Which par-3 hole at Augusta National is famous for producing the most holes-in-one during Masters practice rounds due to a 'skipping' tradition?
sports.fact: Players skip balls across the pond on the 16th during practice — and some accidentally hole out doing it!

food.question: Which spice was once so valuable in the Banda Islands that the Dutch traded Manhattan to the British in exchange for control of its production?
food.fact: The 1667 Treaty of Breda saw the Dutch swap Manhattan for the nutmeg-rich island of Run.

food.question: True or False: Saffron threads are actually the dried stigmas of a type of crocus flower, and each flower produces only three stigmas.
options: True, but each flower produces six stigmas
True, but they are the petals, not stigmas
food.fact: It takes roughly 75,000 crocus flowers to produce just one pound of saffron spice.

food.question: In 2025, researchers published findings that which common herb contains compounds shown to significantly inhibit tau protein aggregation linked to Alzheimer's?
food.fact: Carnosic acid in rosemary showed remarkable neuroprotective effects in the 2025 study.

food.question: What spice gets its intense red color from the compound capsanthin rather than capsaicin, making it colorful but not necessarily hot?
food.fact: Sweet paprika varieties contain almost no capsaicin but are among the most vibrantly red spices.

food.question: Which plant-based protein source is made by washing wheat flour dough until all the starch is removed, leaving only the gluten?
food.fact: Seitan was invented by Buddhist monks in China over 1,500 years ago as a meat substitute.

food.question: How many liters of water does it take on average to produce a single kilogram of almonds, making them one of the most water-intensive vegan foods?
food.fact: A single almond requires roughly 12 liters of water — California grows 80% of the world's supply.

food.question: Which fermented vegan condiment, traditionally Japanese, is made from soybeans and a grain inoculated with Aspergillus oryzae mold?
food.fact: Some miso varieties are aged for over three years, developing complex umami flavors like fine wine.

food.question: Which Italian pasta shape's name literally translates to 'little worms'?
food.fact: Despite the unappetizing name, vermicelli is beloved worldwide and predates spaghetti in Italian cuisine.

food.question: In traditional Italian cuisine, what does the term 'aglio e olio' refer to as the two primary ingredients of the dish?
food.fact: This Neapolitan midnight dish was historically called 'the poor man's pasta' using only pantry staples.

food.question: Which Italian cheese is traditionally buried in clay pots and left to ferment with live insect larvae on the island of Sardinia?
food.fact: Casu marzu contains live maggots that can jump 15 centimeters — diners sometimes wear eye protection.

music.question: Which jazz pianist was known for arriving late to gigs and once pawned a club's piano to buy heroin?
music.fact: Bud Powell's erratic behavior stemmed partly from a 1945 police beating that caused lasting brain damage.

music.question: In blues music, what tuning—where all six open strings form a single chord—did Skip James famously use in 'Devil Got My Woman'?
music.fact: Skip James's haunting open D minor tuning was so unusual that almost no other Delta blues artist adopted it.

music.question: True or False: Miles Davis's 'Kind of Blue' was recorded almost entirely in first takes with minimal rehearsal.
options: Only the first side was first takes
Only 'So What' was a first take
music.fact: Davis intentionally gave musicians sketches instead of full scores, wanting spontaneous performances on the spot.

music.question: Which jazz saxophonist's 2025 album won the Grammy for Best Jazz Instrumental Album at the 2026 ceremony?
music.fact: Immanuel Wilkins has been hailed as one of the most important alto saxophonists of his generation.

music.question: Dexys Midnight Runners had a massive hit with 'Come On Eileen' in 1982. What was the band's original musical style before that song?
music.fact: Their debut album 'Searching for the Young Soul Rebels' was a raw northern soul record, nothing like 'Eileen.'

music.question: Which one-hit wonder's 'Take On Me' failed on its first release in 1984 before topping charts with a reissue featuring a new music video?
music.fact: The original 1984 version flopped completely; the iconic pencil-sketch animated video turned it into a global smash.

music.question: Chesney Hawkes is known for which 1991 one-hit wonder, written by The Who's Pete Townshend?
options: I Am the One and Only
music.fact: Despite Pete Townshend writing it and Chesney being a famous actor's son, he never had another hit.

music.question: Which K-pop group's member, Suga (BTS), released a solo documentary concert film in 2023 that grossed over $40 million worldwide?
music.fact: Suga performed under his rap alias Agust D, which is 'DT Suga' reversed—DT standing for Daegu Town.

music.question: Which Japanese city-pop song from 1979 went viral on YouTube in the 2020s, accumulating hundreds of millions of views decades after release?
options: Stay With Me by Miki Matsubara
Ride on Time by Tatsuro Yamashita
music.fact: YouTube's algorithm mysteriously began recommending 'Plastic Love' to millions around 2017, baffling Takeuchi herself.

music.question: How many members does the K-pop group SEVENTEEN have?
music.fact: Despite the name, SEVENTEEN has 13 members divided into 3 units—the numbers 13+3+1 group equals 17.

technology.question: What was the name of the first neural network architecture to use 'attention is all you need' as its foundational principle, replacing recurrence entirely?
technology.fact: The 2017 Transformer paper's title 'Attention Is All You Need' became one of the most cited phrases in AI history.

technology.question: In 2025, which company released a model that could generate full-length, coherent videos from text prompts lasting over a minute?
technology.fact: Sora stunned researchers by generating physically plausible scenes that earlier models couldn't handle at all.

technology.question: True or False: The original AlphaGo system that defeated Lee Sedol in 2016 was initially trained on human game data before self-play.
options: True — it used human games first, then self-play
False — it was entirely self-taught from scratch
True — but only for the opening moves
False — it only used Monte Carlo tree search
technology.fact: Only the later AlphaGo Zero was purely self-taught; the original AlphaGo studied 160,000 human games first.

technology.question: Which machine learning technique deliberately adds noise to training data during the diffusion process, then learns to reverse it to generate new samples?
technology.fact: DDPMs were considered too slow to be practical until 2020, then rapidly overtook GANs for image generation quality.

technology.question: Which tech billionaire sold his first company, Zip2, to Compaq in 1999 for approximately $307 million?
technology.fact: Musk personally earned about $22 million from the Zip2 sale — his first major windfall before PayPal.

technology.question: Who became the youngest self-made billionaire in tech history at age 23, surpassing Mark Zuckerberg's previous record?
technology.fact: Alexandr Wang founded Scale AI at age 19; by 2022 the defense-and-AI data firm made him a billionaire at 25 — though Austin Russell hit it at 25 too.

technology.question: Which reclusive tech billionaire co-founded the database giant Oracle but spent years living on the Hawaiian island of Lanai, which he purchased in 2012?
technology.fact: Ellison bought 98% of Lanai for an estimated $300 million and has been transforming it into a sustainability lab.

technology.question: What was the original name of Microsoft's first operating system, purchased from Seattle Computer Products in 1981?
technology.fact: QDOS stood for 'Quick and Dirty Operating System' — Microsoft bought it for just $50,000 and resold it to IBM.

technology.question: How many years did Bill Gates serve as CEO of Microsoft before stepping down from the role?
technology.fact: Gates was CEO from Microsoft's 1975 founding until January 2000 — but remained chairman until 2014.

technology.question: Which Microsoft product, launched in 2003 and discontinued in 2015, was an early attempt at a social networking and blogging platform?
technology.fact: Windows Live Spaces once had 120 million users but couldn't compete with Facebook — Microsoft migrated users to WordPress.

nature.question: Which coral species is known as 'the cockroach of the reef' for its remarkable ability to survive bleaching events that kill most other corals?
nature.fact: Porites lobata can survive extreme heat and acidification that devastates faster-growing coral species nearby.

nature.question: In 2025, researchers off the coast of Tahiti discovered an unusually pristine deep reef ecosystem at what surprising depth?
nature.fact: These mesophotic 'twilight zone' reefs may serve as refuges for coral species fleeing warming shallow waters.

nature.question: What percentage of the ocean floor do coral reefs cover, despite supporting roughly 25% of all marine species?
nature.fact: Coral reefs cover just 0.1% of the ocean floor yet host a quarter of all known marine fish species.

nature.question: True or False: Some species of coral can actually walk across the ocean floor, moving several centimeters per day.
options: True — mushroom corals can flip and crawl
False — no coral species can move at all
True — but only during larval stages
False — they drift only via ocean currents
nature.fact: Free-living mushroom corals (Fungiidae) inflate their tissues to flip over and slowly crawl across sediment.

nature.question: Which great ape has been documented using medicinal plants to treat wounds, with a 2024 study showing a Sumatran orangutan applying a specific plant poultice to a facial wound?
nature.fact: The orangutan chewed leaves of Fibraurea tinctoria, a plant with known antibacterial properties, and applied it directly.

nature.question: How many species of great apes (excluding humans) are currently recognized by the IUCN?
nature.fact: The seven species include two orangutans, two gorillas, chimpanzee, bonobo, and the Tapanuli orangutan described in 2017.

nature.question: Which primate is the only known non-human species to consistently communicate using a syntax-like structure combining different alarm calls into meaningful sequences?
nature.fact: Campbell's monkeys add suffixes to root calls, changing meaning — a rudimentary form of grammatical structure.

nature.question: The wood frog (Rana sylvatica) survives winter by doing something that would kill most vertebrates. What is it?
options: Allowing up to 65% of its body water to freeze solid
Entering a coma-like state with no heartbeat for 2+ years
nature.fact: Wood frogs produce glucose antifreeze, letting ice crystals form between cells while vital organs remain protected.

nature.question: Which venomous lizard, native to Mexico, produces a compound in its saliva that became the basis for a widely prescribed Type 2 diabetes drug?
nature.fact: Exendin-4 from Mexican beaded lizard saliva led to exenatide (Byetta), a blockbuster diabetes medication.

nature.question: The tuatara of New Zealand resembles a lizard but belongs to an entirely separate reptilian order. Approximately how long ago did its lineage diverge from other reptiles?
nature.fact: Tuataras are the sole survivors of order Rhynchocephalia, making them more ancient than most dinosaur lineages.

film.question: Which film holds the record for most Oscar nominations without a single win?
options: Both The Turning Point and The Color Purple
film.fact: Both films received 11 nominations each and went home completely empty-handed on Oscar night.

film.question: At the 2025 Academy Awards, which film won Best International Feature Film?
options: The Seed of the Sacred Fig
film.fact: Brazil's 'I'm Still Here' by Walter Salles marked the country's first Best International Feature win.

film.question: Who is the only person to have been nominated for acting Oscars in five consecutive decades?
film.fact: Streep's nominations span the 1970s through the 2010s, with 21 acting nominations total.

film.question: True or False: The envelope mix-up at the 2017 Oscars happened because a PricewaterhouseCoopers accountant was tweeting backstage.
options: It was a planned publicity stunt
film.fact: Accountant Brian Cullinan was photographed tweeting a backstage photo of Emma Stone moments before the mix-up.

film.question: Which action star performed a real HALO jump from 25,000 feet for 'Mission: Impossible – Fallout'?
film.fact: Cruise became the first actor to perform an actual HALO jump on camera, requiring over 100 skydives to film.

film.question: Before becoming an action icon, which martial arts star worked as a janitor and dishwasher after immigrating to the United States?
film.fact: Bruce Lee worked as a restaurant waiter and taught cha-cha dancing in Seattle before his film career.

film.question: In 'Die Hard,' what alias does Hans Gruber use when he pretends to be a hostage to fool John McClane?
film.fact: Alan Rickman improvised the American accent for the 'Bill Clay' scene, impressing both cast and crew.

film.question: Which arthouse director cast non-professional actors exclusively in his Palme d'Or-winning 'The Tree of Wooden Clogs' (1978)?
film.fact: Olmi used real Bergamo peasants speaking their local dialect, many of whom had never seen a movie.

film.question: Which independent film was shot over 12 years with the same cast, documenting a boy's journey from age 6 to 18?
film.fact: Richard Linklater filmed 'Boyhood' annually from 2002 to 2013, a unprecedented approach in narrative cinema.

film.question: Iranian director Abbas Kiarostami's 'Close-Up' (1990) blurs documentary and fiction by having real people reenact what event?
film.fact: A real man impersonated director Mohsen Makhmalbaf to a family, and everyone reenacted the actual court case on film.

politics.question: Which small EU member state held the rotating presidency of the Council of the European Union in the first half of 2025?
politics.fact: Poland held the EU Council presidency from January to June 2025, its second time ever.

politics.question: In the European Parliament, what is the minimum number of member states required to form a political group?
politics.fact: Since 2024 rules, an EP political group needs at least 23 MEPs from at least 4 member states.

politics.question: Which EU institution has the exclusive right to propose new legislation, a power sometimes called the 'right of initiative'?
politics.fact: Unlike most democracies where parliaments propose laws, the EU Parliament cannot initiate legislation itself.

politics.question: True or False: Denmark has a permanent opt-out from the euro but voted in 2022 to end its opt-out from EU defense policy.
politics.fact: Russia's invasion of Ukraine prompted Danes to abolish their 30-year defense opt-out in a June 2022 referendum.

politics.question: Which leader's 1963 speech in West Berlin included words in German that became one of the Cold War's most iconic phrases?
politics.fact: JFK's 'Ich bin ein Berliner' phrase was grammatically debated but perfectly understood by the ecstatic crowd.

politics.question: Winston Churchill's famous 'Iron Curtain' speech was delivered in 1946 at a small college in which U.S. state?
politics.fact: Churchill spoke at Westminster College in Fulton, Missouri — a school with fewer than 1,000 students.

politics.question: Who delivered the 'Winds of Change' speech to the South African Parliament in 1960, signaling Britain's shift on colonial Africa?
politics.fact: Macmillan's speech stunned the apartheid government; many white South African MPs sat in hostile silence.

politics.question: What was Operation Gladio, revealed publicly in 1990, in the context of Cold War Europe?
options: A NATO network of secret stay-behind armies
A CIA plan to assassinate Castro
A British intelligence operation in East Berlin
politics.fact: NATO secretly armed clandestine paramilitary networks across Western Europe to resist potential Soviet occupation.

politics.question: Which neutral European country secretly allowed U.S. spy planes, including U-2 flights, to use its airspace during the Cold War despite its official neutrality?
politics.fact: Officially neutral Sweden had extensive secret military cooperation with NATO throughout the entire Cold War.

politics.question: How many nuclear weapons did the Soviet Union detonate in the 'Tsar Bomba' test of 1961?
politics.fact: Tsar Bomba's shockwave circled Earth three times; windows broke 560 miles away from the detonation.
